topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a gavel strike. My stomach plummeted, a cold, sickening drop that stole my breath. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling so badly the mouse danced erratically across the screen. The headline screamed my name, the content detailed my meticulous research, my unique perspective, my very voice. But the byline? It read 'Elias Thorne.' Elias Thorne. A stranger. I reread the text, the familiar cadence of my arguments now belonging to someone else, a ghost wearing my skin. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room spin. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. My hands clenched into tight fists at my sides, knuckles white. I needed air, needed distance, but the air felt too thin, too charged with a sudden, sharp sense of being utterly exposed.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred into meaningless shapes. The notification blinked insistently, a digital accusation. It was a published piece, a piece I had poured months of sleepless nights into crafting, now sitting under a different name. A strange, hollow feeling settled deep in my chest, a tightness that made drawing a full breath an effort. I walked to the window, the city lights outside seeming too bright, too indifferent to the turmoil churning inside me. My jaw ached, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically rub away the phantom sensation of being unseen, of having my labor stolen. Every nerve ending felt hypersensitive, waiting for the next blow. The silence of my apartment amplified the frantic thumping in my ears.

[story 3]

When I finally looked up from the monitor, the words swam before my eyes. The realization hit not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, creeping frost. It was a betrayal, cold and sharp, slicing through the warmth I had felt moments before. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning beneath my weight, and let out a sound that was halfway between a sigh and a choked gasp. My throat felt constricted, dry and tight. I picked up my phone, my movements slow and deliberate, as if moving through thick water. I scrolled through my contacts, the familiar faces suddenly seeming alien, distant. A profound sense of disorientation gripped me; the ground beneath my feet felt unsteady, and for a moment, I couldn't remember how to anchor myself.
